subsection.heading: Identification of long-distance trails
subsection.chapeau: Not later than 18 months after January 4, 2025, the Secretaries shall identify—

paragraph.content: not fewer than 10 long-distance bike trails that make use of trails and roads in existence on January 4, 2025; and
not fewer than 10 areas in which there is an opportunity to develop or complete a trail that would qualify as a long-distance bike trail.
develop a process to allow members of the public to comment regarding the identification of trails and areas under subsection (a); and
consider the identification, development, and completion of long-distance bike trails in a geographically equitable manner.

subsection.heading: Maps, signage, and promotional materials
subsection.chapeau: For any long-distance bike trail identified under subsection (a), the Secretary concerned may—

paragraph.content: publish and distribute maps, install signage, and issue promotional materials; and
coordinate with stakeholders to leverage any non-Federal resources necessary for the stewardship, development, or completion of trails.

content.p: Not later than 2 years after January 4, 2025, the Secretaries, in partnership with interested organizations, shall prepare and publish a report that lists the trails identified under subsection (a), including a summary of public comments received in accordance with the process developed under subsection (b).

subsection.heading: Conflict avoidance with other uses
subsection.chapeau: Before identifying a long-distance bike trail under subsection (a), the Secretary concerned shall ensure the long-distance bike trail—

subparagraph.content: the uses, before January 4, 2025, of any trail or road that is part of that long-distance bike trail;
multiple-use areas where biking, hiking, horseback riding, or use by pack and saddle stock are existing uses on January 4, 2025;
the purposes for which any trail was or is established under the National Trails System Act (16 U.S.C. 1241 et seq.); and
any area managed under the Wilderness Act (16 U.S.C. 1131 et seq.); and

paragraph.content: complies with land use and management plans of the Federal recreational lands and waters that are part of that long-distance bike trail.

content.p: In carrying out this section, the Secretaries may not use eminent domain or condemnation.

paragraph.heading: Long-distance bike trail
paragraph.chapeau: The term “long-distance bike trail” means a continuous route, consisting of 1 or more trails or rights-of-way, that—

subparagraph.content: is not less than 80 miles in length;
primarily makes use of dirt or natural surface trails;
may require connections along paved or other improved roads;
does not include Federal recreational lands where mountain biking or related activities are not consistent with management requirements for those Federal recreational lands; and
to the maximum extent practicable, makes use of trails and roads that were on Federal recreational lands on or before January 4, 2025.

content.p: The term “Secretaries” means the Secretary of the Interior and the Secretary of Agriculture, acting jointly.

note.heading: References in Text
note.p: The National Trails System Act, referred to in subsec. (e)(1)(C), is Pub. L. 90–543, Oct. 2, 1968, 82 Stat. 919, which is classified generally to chapter 27 (§ 1241 et seq.) of this title. For complete classification of this Act to the Code, see Short Title note set out under section 1241 of this title and Tables.

The Wilderness Act, referred to in subsec. (e)(1)(D), is Pub. L. 88–577, Sept. 3, 1964, 78 Stat. 890, which is classified generally to chapter 23 (§ 1131 et seq.) of this title. For complete classification of this Act to the Code, see Short Title note set out under section 1131 of this title and Tables.
